coredata.dc:title: The snow samples waste tobacco leaves-derived carbon dots for broad-spectrum and sensitive extraction of organophosphate esters

Hu, Xingru
Sun, Shihao
Liu, Shan
Liang, Taibo

Zhang, Weichen
lei, Yu
Han, Lin
Xie, Jianping
coredata.dc:description: The increasing organophosphate esters (OPEs) consumption poses a significant threat to human well-being. The OPEs’ sample pretreatment is often challenging due to their distinct physical and chemical properties. Herein, carbon quantum dots derived from waste tobacco leaves (WTL-CQDs) were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method and employed as solid-phase microextraction (SPME) fiber coating for OPEs extraction. Coated fibers exhibited a broad-spectrum and sensitive performance for OPE extraction due to strong hydrogen bonds between amino and hydroxyl groups on WTL-CQDs and P = O groups in OPEs. The density functional theory (DFT) calculations revealed that the TD-CQDs binding energy and three alkyl, halogenated and aromatic substituted OPEs is −16 ∼ -18 kJ/mol. When coupled with a gas chromatography-flame photometric detector (GC-FPD), the developed method demonstrated a wide linear range (2–2000 ng/L) and low detection limits (0.01–20 ng/L). The proposed technique effectively detected trace OPEs in real snow meltwater samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid phase microextraction
Organic phosphate esters
Carbon quantum dots, waste tobacco leaves and density functional theory calculations

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Comparison of WTL-CQDs-SPMEs fibers with commercial fibers
Evaluation of green character of the method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the WTL-CQDs preparation processes, the preparation of WTL-CQDs-SPME fibers and SPME analysis.

(a) HRTEM image of CQDs and corresponding particle size histogram, (b) Particle size distribution of CQDs, (c) XRD patterns of CQDs, (d) TG curves of WTL-CQDs-SPME fiber, (e)and (f) SEM images of lateral and cross-sections of WTL-CQDs-SPME fibers.
Structural characterization of CQDs. (a) FT-IR, (b) XPS, (c) high-resolution C1s, (d) high-resolution N1s, (e) high-resolution O1s, (f) 1H NMR, (g) 13C NMR and (h) WTL-CQDs structural formula.

Optimization of critical factors influencing the SPME extraction efficiency and comparison of extraction efficiency between commercial SPME fibers: (a) extraction temperature, (b) extraction time, (c) desorption temperature, (d) desorption time, (e) pH and (f) salt concentration. (g) Comparison of extraction efficiency between commercial SPME fibers for nine OPEs detection.
Theoretical analysis of weak interactions: (a) TCPP, (b) TEP, (c) TMP and (d) TPHP.
Analytical performance of WTL-CQDs-SPME fiber for OPEs extraction.
Comparison of the present work with other similar methods used for extraction and determination of OPEs.
Analytical results for detecting OPEs in actual snow meltwater samples.

head.ce:title: The snow samples waste tobacco leaves-derived carbon dots for broad-spectrum and sensitive extraction of organophosphate esters

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhengzhou Tobacco Research Institute of CNTC

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing organophosphate esters (OPEs) consumption poses a significant threat to human well-being. The OPEs’ sample pretreatment is often challenging due to their distinct physical and chemical properties. Herein, carbon quantum dots derived from waste tobacco leaves (WTL-CQDs) were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method and employed as solid-phase microextraction (SPME) fiber coating for OPEs extraction. Coated fibers exhibited a broad-spectrum and sensitive performance for OPE extraction due to strong hydrogen bonds between amino and hydroxyl groups on WTL-CQDs and P = O groups in OPEs. The density functional theory (DFT) calculations revealed that the TD-CQDs binding energy and three alkyl, halogenated and aromatic substituted OPEs is −16 ∼ -18 kJ/mol. When coupled with a gas chromatography-flame photometric detector (GC-FPD), the developed method demonstrated a wide linear range (2–2000 ng/L) and low detection limits (0.01–20 ng/L). The proposed technique effectively detected trace OPEs in real snow meltwater samples.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Carbon quantum dots, waste tobacco leaves and density functional theory calculations

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphate esters (OPEs) are synthetic phosphoric acid derivatives with a wide broader application range prospects in herbicides, fungicides, flame retardants and plasticizers in various commercial products such as textiles, electronics, building materials and polyurethane foam [1]. OPEs are not chemically bonded to polymers in these with other materials in the product and are typically incorporated through physical mixing. Consequently, they are easily released at high concentrations ubiquitous into the environment including soil, waste water, air and foodstuffs human blood through mechanical abrasion, leaching and volatilization, causing substantial threats to human health [2–4]. Ecological risk assessment research suggested proved that OPEs exhibit potential neurotoxicity, carcinogenicity, mutagenicity and immunotoxin. Moreover, OPEs are already ubiquitous in the environment [5,6]. Environmental samples are typically intricate, and OPE concentrations are generally low, posing a significant challenge in direct analysis using conventional analytical techniques. Sample preparation is crucial in enhancing their detection sensitivity and minimizing matrix interference. However, the diverse structural substitutions in OPEs alter their chemical and physical properties, ranging from strong polarity to strong hydrophobicity [7]. When these OPEs, as trace contaminants, are co-located in complex environmental samples, selecting an appropriate sample preparation method becomes essential in the analysis [8,9].

Solid phase microextraction (SPME) is a green analytical technique integrating sampling, extraction, concentration and injection. SPME offers many advantages, such as simple operation, time-saving and environmental friendliness. The fundamental principle of SPME is the distribution equilibrium between the sample matrix and extractive phase coating. Therefore, the coating adsorbent properties are decisive in enhancing the method's analytical performance, especially in determining its sensitivity.[10–13].

Several commercial and homemade fibers have been recently developed and effectively applied to analyze trace targets, including OPEs. Chen et al. [14] synthesized three graphene oxide (GO)--based molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) fibers, demonstrating a high binding capacity for target analytes due to enhanced selectivity and improved extraction efficiency. Fei et al. [15] used urea and H2O2 to synthesize an oxygen-doped graphitic carbon nitride (O-g-C3N4) featuring a rich porous structure and surface functional groups. These attributes facilitated the headspace SPME of five moderately polar and non-polar OPEs in environmental samples. Nonetheless, these sorbents exhibited poor extraction efficiency for target molecule pollutants (TMPs), involved multiple synthesis steps, and required templates and toxic solvents, limiting their practical application for OPEs' extraction [13]. Therefore, developing novel coatings to enrich broad-spectrum OPEs efficiently remains challenging.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have gained increasing popularity in biosensing, medical imaging and environmental pollution monitoring due to their excellent biocompatibility, large specific surface area, abundant raw materials and remarkable fluorescence properties [16–19]. CQDs are sp2/sp3 hybrid carbon atoms with π-π stacking interactions between benzene ring and C = C double bond in organic analytes and hydrophobic interaction with non-polar alkyl groups. The CQD surface mainly contains oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, lactone and acid anhydride) that can form hydrogen bonds with polar analytes [20,21]. The exceptional properties of CQDs enable them to have a broad potential application range in broad-spectrum extraction capacity.

Tobacco, a member of the Solanaceae plant family, has abundant alkaloid content. The waste tobacco leaves generated by the flue-cured tobacco industry are utilized as biomass raw materials for CQDs production. This approach conserves resources and yields CQDs with rich amino, hydroxyl and other functional groups to enhance adsorption capabilities for pollutants with diverse chemical structures[22,23]. Hence, exploring waste tobacco leaves-derived carbon quantum dots (WTL-CQDs) as an SPME fiber coating for OPEs remains highly desirable.

In this research, novel WTL-CQDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method and then employed as SPME coating adsorbents. Their morphology, chemical structure and fluorescence response were characterized. The WTL-CQDs' extraction ability was investigated by selecting nine OPEs with different polarities and structures (Listed in Table S1). Compared to commercial fibers, the WTL-CQDs coated fibers exhibited exceptional extraction performance. According to DFT calculations, amino and hydroxyl groups on WTL-CQDs favored the sensitive and broad-spectrum extraction of OPEs. Finally, the established SPME-GC-FPD method was successfully employed for trace OPE analysis in snow meltwater samples.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without additional purification. Tobacco leaves were provided by the Zhengzhou Tobacco Research Institute. Potassium bromide (>99.9 %) and quinine sulfate (>99.0 %) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Network. Organophosphate mixtures (TMP, TEP, TnBP, TcEP, TPHP, TCrP, TCPP, TDCPP and EHDPP), with a concentration of 250 ppm, were obtained from Dr. Ehrenstorfer, Germany, mixed with ultrapure H2O at 1:1 ratio.

Waste tobacco leaves were dried at 100 °C in an oven and then crushed into fine powder. 1.5 g tobacco powder and 47 mL deionized H2O were weighed and placed in a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor. Then, the reaction vessel was placed in the oven and hydrothermally reacted at 180 ∼ 220 °C for 4 ∼ 12 h. The reaction solution was cooled, filtered and washed with deionized H2O three times, and the supernatant was obtained for freeze-drying to obtain brown WTL-CQDs.

Various characterization techniques were employed for this study. For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis, the sample was ultrasonically dispersed in H2O at 1 mg mL−1 concentration, deposited on a copper grid, and analyzed on a JEM-2100 microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer) was used to assess the crystallinity of the synthesized sample. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to analyze the functional groups using a KBr method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to determine the elemental composition using a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy (Bruker 600 MHz system) examined the chemical structure of carbon quantum dots.

The WTL-CQD coated fiber preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 . The WTL-CQD powder is attached to a stainless-steel fiber by adhesion. First, the stainless-steel wire (φ 0.30 mm) is pretreated in hydrofluoric acid and acetone for 20 min each. Then, a neutral silicone and toluene mixture at a 1:2 mass volume ratio was prepared. The dried stainless steel wire was immersed in the adhesive solution. After wiping with filter papers, the wire was coated with WTL-CQD powder by rotating it. Subsequently, the fiber was cured at 120 °C for 30 min.

Before analysis, the WTL-CQD coated fiber was inserted in the GC injection port to remove impurities. The fiber was directly immersed in the OPE standard solution to investigate its extraction performance.

The extracted samples were analyzed on a 7890B gas chromatograph (Agilent Technologies, USA) equipped with a splitless injector. Analytes were separated and detected by HP-5 capillary column (30 m × 0.32 mm, 0.25 µm) and flame photometric detector (FPD). The oven temperature was programmed as follows: The initial temperature was 80 °C (held for 1 min), then increased to 140 °C (kept for 2 min) at 20 °C min−1, and then further increased to 280 °C (kept 6 min) at 4 °C min−1. The temperature of the injector and detector was 270 °C and 250 °C, respectively. The detector temperature was set at 250℃. Helium (99.999 %) was used as carrier gas at a constant flow rate of 1.5 mL min−1, and nitrogen (99.999 %) was used as supplementary gas at a flow rate of 20 mL min−1. Synthetic air (99.995 %) and hydrogen (99.999 %) were used as detection gases with flow rates of 100 mL min−1 and 65 mL min−1, respectively.

The snow samples were collected from three cities, including Daqing in northeast China, Beijing in north China and Zhengzhou in central China. Their geographical locations are depicted in Fig. S1 . The snow was collected from residential and on-campus areas in Daqing, Beijing and Zhengzhou. Snow samples were dissolved in H2O and extracted using the WTL-CQDs coated SPME fibers without additional treatment. A standard 100 ug L-1 concentration sample was added to the environmental sample to calculate recovery.

TEM analysis is taken to characterize the WTL-CQDs morphology. WTL-CQDs are uniformly dispersed without noticeable aggregation, and their diameter is 1 ∼ 4 nm with an average size of 2.02 nm (Fig. 2
a). HRTEM images further illustrate distinct lattice fringes with a 0.31 nm spacing. The XRD spectrum peaks at 2θ = 21° indicate an increased layer spacing (Fig. 2c) caused by the steric hindrance from functional groups at the edges of sp2 carbon plane or the distortion of sp3 hybridized carbon within sp2 domains. The increased layer spacing offers spatial protection for amino and hydroxyl groups, enhancing their interactions with P = O moieties in OPEs.

The coating wettability is assessed by water contact angle (WCA), suggesting that WTL-CQDs exhibit varying degrees of hydrophobicity with contact angles ranging from 36.35° to 44.45° for materials synthesized at different hydrothermal reaction times (Fig. S3). Additionally, ultraviolet (UV) absorption and photoluminescence (PL) measurements reveal a significant increase in intensity with longer reaction times, likely due to increased defects in WTL-CQDs (Fig. S4). This variation in contact angles and fluorescence intensity reflects that the hydrothermal reaction time profoundly affects the CQDs' chemical structure and surface wettability. Longer reaction times promote complex surface functionalization, altering the interaction of polar functional groups. Therefore, the WTL-CQDs synthesized at 180 °C for 10 h are selected for subsequent investigations.

Thermogravimetric analysis is conducted to investigate further the stability and functional properties of WTL-CQDs (Fig. 2d). These results display that WTL-CQD coating maintains good thermal stability below 200 °C, making it suitable for SPME fiber coatings. The surface morphology of SPME fiber coating is analyzed using SEM (Fig. 2e-2f). These images indicate that the fiber is uniformly covered with a homogeneous WTL-CQD layer, forming a thick film with an 181 μm thickness.

FT-IR spectroscopy is performed to determine the WTL-CQDs surface groups. Fig. 3
a reveals that the broad and strong band at 3100 ∼ 3600 cm−1 is derived from the O-H and N-H stretching vibrations. Strong peaks at 1500 and 1800 cm−1 are attributed to the C = O stretching vibration in carbonyl compounds and C = C, the stretching vibration of the aromatic ring skeleton, respectively. Absorption peaks at 1340 and 1480 cm−1 are assigned to the O-H bond bending vibration and the C-N stretching vibration in amide and nicotine. FT-IR analysis of WTL-CQDs prepared at different hydrothermal reaction times is also performed (Fig. S2). The WTL-CQDs curves indicate that the functional groups in tobacco leaves are retained in WTL-CQDs. Fig. 3 b depicts the XPS spectrum of WTL-CQDs. The full spectrum display the peaks of Si2p (102.08 eV), C1s (284.08 eV), N1s (399.58 eV) and O1s (532.08 eV), indicating that WTL-CQDs are mainly composed of C (69.77 %), N (5.44 %), O (22.53 %), Si (2.56 %) and relatively small silicon (N) content. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s indicates (Fig. 3c) its three components, namely C-C (284.81 eV), C-O (286.08 eV) and C = O (288.39 eV). The N1s peak is deconvoluted into C-N (399.44 eV), N-H (400.17 eV), graphite nitrogen (401.52 eV) and N = O (402.08 eV) peaks (Fig. 3d). The O1s peak is split into C = O (531.84 eV) and C-O (533.01 eV), as illustrated in Fig. 3e. The above results show that hydroxyl, carboxyl, ester, amino and amide groups functionalize the sp2 carbon domain of WTL-CQDs.

The structure of WTL-CQDs is further characterized by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. Fig. 3f displays the 1H NMR spectra of WTL-CQDs (600 MHz, CDCl3): δ 0.88 (t, 1, J = 6.8 Hz, CH2CH3), 2.09 (s, 2, OC2H3), 2.43 (s, 2, C5NH4C4H7NCH3), 3.66–3.87 (d, 2, J = 5.3 Hz, C5NH4C4H7NCH3), 5.30 (s, 1, CH2OH), 8.59 (s, 1, NH2). Fig. 3g illustrates the 13C NMR of WTL-CQDs (150 MHz, CDCl3): δ 22.01, 29.85, 39.18, 56.23, 61.93, 124.49, 136.28, 149.68, 158.00, 166.68, 185.35, 198.21.

Based on these structural characterization results and previously reported literature [24–27], a representative molecular structural model is proposed for WTL-CQDs, comprising 18 fused aromatic rings functionalized with edge groups and condensed aromatic hydrocarbons (Fig. 3 h). This data suggests that WTL-CQDs is a complex mixture of conjugated sp2 carbon (dense aromatic rings) with similar structures and varying sizes.

Setting SPME extraction parameters is crucial for robust experimental results. Since SPME cannot extract 100 % of analytes, operating conditions should be strictly controlled to ensure the accuracy of the experimental data. Therefore, the impact of extraction temperature, extraction time, resolution temperature, resolution time, pH and salt concentration on WTL-CQDs-SPME fibers' performance is investigated.

Temperature is an important parameter that shortens the equilibration time and accelerates the extraction process. The optimal extraction temperature is determined by measuring the adsorption intensity of the analyte after adsorbing it at different extraction temperatures. Fig. 4
a depicts the adsorption intensity of 9 organophosphates at 20 ∼ 60°C. The extraction efficiency is highest at 60 °C. The extraction time is another crucial factor that affects the extraction efficiency. Therefore, the adsorption intensity is further investigated at an extraction time of 1 ∼ 50 min (Fig. 4 b). When WTL-CQDs-SPMES is used as an extraction fiber, the optimal extraction time of the nine analytes is reached at 30 min.

The desorption temperature and time also affect the resolution of the analyte from SPME fiber. The desorption efficiency of OPEs is investigated from WTL-CQDs-SPMEs fibers in the temperature range of 230 ∼ 280 °C and the desorption time range of 1 ∼ 6 min. Fig. 4 c and 4d display that the desorption temperature of 260 °C and a desorption time of 6 min realizes the best desorption effect.

The pH can influence the analytical results by changing a target analyte's ionization or protonation state and modifying the protonation of hydroxyl and amino groups on WTL-CQDs. The pH in the range of 2 to 12 is optimized. Fig. 4 e indicates that the analyte detection intensity remains stable in acidic and neutral conditions. However, the intensity significantly decreases when pH≧8 is used, which is attributed to the deprotonation of hydroxyl groups on WTL-CQDs in an alkaline environment, forming hydroxyl ions and reducing the binding strength. The concentration of salt ions in the sample matrix also plays a crucial role in determining the extraction efficiency. Increased salt concentration can cause a “salting out” phenomenon where the analytes' solubility in the aqueous phase decreases, thereby enhancing their partitioning in the SPME fiber coating. Fig. 4f illustrates the investigation of salt concentration ranging from 0 % to 25 % (m/v). The extraction efficiency improved in the range of 0 % to 5 %. Therefore, subsequent experiments are conducted using a 5 % NaCl solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of WTL-CQDs-SPMEs fibers with commercial fibers
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the extraction performance of WTL-CQDs-SPMEs fibers, a comparison with standard commercial fibers is carried out under optimal conditions, such as 75 μm carboxen/polydimethyisiloxane fiber (CAR/PDMS), 65 μm PDMS/DVA fiber and 100 μm PDMS fiber. The extraction efficiency of WTL-CQDs-SPMEs fiber is significantly higher than CAR/PDMS, PDMS/DVA and PDMS-SPMEs fibers (Fig. 4 g). The WTL-CQDs-SPMEs fiber performed remarkably in all selected OPEs, including polar and non-polar species. In contrast, the adsorption performance of three commercial fibers for polar organophosphates (TMP and TEP) is poor, with the PDMS-SPME fiber exhibiting negligible adsorption for TMP.

During extraction, a high electron cloud density of phosphorus-oxygen double bonds in OPE molecules facilitates the effective attraction of hydrogen atoms, resulting in a reaction with amino or hydroxyl groups at the edge of the carbon dot structure. This reaction remains uninfluenced by the substituent structure in OPEs. The change in carbon dot structure can promote the exposure of more active sites, and the steric hindrance of surface functional groups forms a “spatial protection” of hydrogen bonds, thereby improving the extraction ability. Therefore, it can be inferred that the designed SPME coating is feasible for broad-spectrum and highly sensitive extraction of OPEs in environmental water samples. Theoretical calculations are conducted to validate the hypothesis further, and the weak interaction between OPEs and WTL-CQDs is calculated. Fig. 5 illustrates that the amine groups in WTL-CQDs form stable hydrogen bonds with phosphorus-oxygen double bonds in OPEs of different polarities. Additionally, the stabilization energy shift (ΔEn) is also estimated. All OPEs-WTL-CQDs-SPME samples exhibit negative ΔEn values ( Fig. S5 ), demonstrating supramolecules' stability and spontaneous OPE adsorption on modified fibers. Moreover, the binding energies of alkyl, halogenated and aromatic substituted OPEs, such as TMP, TCPP and TPHP, are −16.28 kJ/mol, −17.59 kJ/mol and −17.07 kJ/mol, respectively. Although the polarity of all three OPEs ranges from strong to weak and the substituents volume is small to large, the binding energy is not much different, suggesting that the combination of polar groups in WTL-CQDs and phosphorus double bonds in OPEs can promote the broad-spectrum extraction of OPEs.

Coupled with GC-FPD, a novel SPME-GC approach for OPE analysis is proposed using WTL-CQDs coated SPME fiber. The analytical performance of this approach is assessed under optimal conditions, and the results are briefly presented in Table 1 . The method exhibits a broad line range and satisfactory coefficient of determination (R2 ≥ 0.995) in the 2–2000 ng/L range. The limits of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) are 0.01–20 ng/L. The method's reproducibility is tested by monitoring the relative standard deviation (RSD) between intraday and interday experiments, ranging from 1.1-3.7 % and 1.7–5.2 %, respectively. These results further confirm that the proposed method of WTL-CQDs-SPME-GC- FPD is suitable for detecting OPEs in an aqueous environment.

Moreover, a recovery experiment is performed to evaluate the practicality and accuracy of the proposed approach in real water samples. Standard solutions of OPEs are added to real water samples at concentrations of 100 ng/L. Table 2 reveals that the recoveries of nine OPEs in snow meltwater samples from the three cities ranged from 91.6-109.6 %, with a satisfactory RSD of < 9.56 %. The WTL-CQDs demonstrated remarkable extraction capabilities for all OPEs, attributed to specific adsorption between P = O groups in OPEs and abundant amino and hydroxyl groups in WTL-CQDs. Therefore, a highly sensitive detection method is developed using WTL-CQDs coated fiber coupled with GC-FPD.

The analytical performance of the developed method is compared with previously reported methods (Table 2). The developed method is green and environmentally friendly, with low LODs and LOQs, good reproducibility, satisfactory recoveries and a broad-spectrum extraction capability. Moreover, the preparation procedure is efficient and facile, demonstrating satisfactory sensitivity, accuracy and precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of green character of the method
ce:section.ce:para: Evaluating the “greenness” of analytical methods is crucial for advancing sustainable scientific practices, minimizing environmental impact, and enhancing laboratory safety. The ComplexGAPI tool has been widely employed in various studies to ensure adherence to green chemistry principles [36–38].

In this work, we assessed the greenness of the proposed method using the ComplexMoGAPI tool, which systematically evaluates analytical protocols by considering aspects such as sampling preparation, solvents, apparatus, and analytical methods [39].

As shown in Fig. S6, the ComplexMoGAPI RGB12 generates a pictogram with six pentagons, each representing distinct greenness criteria, indicated by a color scale of red, yellow, and green. Our findings demonstrate significant environmental advantages: the method eliminates harmful solvents, obviates the need for analyte purification, and poses no health or environmental risks. Additionally, the reusable WLT-CQDs fiber produces no waste and ensures low energy consumption. Notably, the overall greenness score of 91 points reflects the method’s strong alignment with sustainable analytical practices.

Due to its porous structure and low temperatures, snow is capable of effectively capturing organophosphate esters (OPEs) present in the atmosphere [5]. Snow meltwater samples from three cities are collected and analyzed to determine OPE contents in real water samples under optimized experimental conditions. The results indicate that nine target pollutants are detected in snow meltwater samples from Daqing (Northeast China), Beijing (Northern China) and Zhengzhou (Central China), with concentrations ranging from 0.2 to 24.8 ng/L (Table 3. and Fig. S7). Results reveal that Daqing samples in Northeast China exhibit higher levels of TCPP and TCEP, which are used as flame retardants in thermal insulation materials. At the same time, alkyl and aromatic substituted OPEs are present in lower concentrations. The snow samples from Beijing show a higher content of expensive flame retardant TDCPP. Conversely, Zhengzhou's snow samples exhibit significantly higher EHDPP and TCrP concentrations than other cities. These results show that OPE residues are prevalent in water samples. The observed variations in OPE types and concentrations are attributed to several factors. First, differences in industrial activities and levels of urbanization among these cities may result in different OPE sources and their emissions. Climatic conditions, such as temperature and precipitation patterns, influence the OPEs' deposition and persistence in snow. Last, these cities' geographical and environmental contexts, such as land use and vegetation cover, also significantly affect OPE types and concentrations detected in snow [40].

Homemade WTL-CQDs coated fibers are successfully applied for the quantitative analysis of trace OPEs in snow meltwater samples from three cities in China, demonstrating its practical applicability. These results indicate that the proposed method provides essential technical support for the residue detection and pollutant treatment of OPEs in environmental water. This approach repurposes agricultural waste into valuable nanomaterials and supports eco-friendly practices in material utilization.

Shitong Zeng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xingru Hu: Supervision, Methodology. Shihao Sun: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shan Liu: Validation, Investigation. Taibo Liang: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Binbin Lu: Writing – review & editing. Yalong Guo: Data curation. Weichen Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Yu lei: . Lin Han: Methodology. Jianping Xie: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32072344), the Chief Scientist Innovation Projectfrom China National Tobacco Corporation (432022CK0510) for their financial support of our work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112383.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A green approach for organophosphate ester determination in airborne particulate matter: microwave-assisted extraction using hydroalcoholic mixture coupled with solid-phase microextraction gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry
Talanta
